_meta.title: SIMD & Vector ISAs — AVX, SVE, RVV

questions.question: SIMD stands for:
questions.explanation: SIMD means a single instruction operates on multiple data elements in parallel using wide vector registers.

questions.question: An AVX2 YMM register is 256 bits wide. How many single-precision (32-bit) floats can it hold?
questions.explanation: 256 bits / 32 bits per float = 8 floats. Each _mm256_add_ps adds 8 pairs of floats in one instruction.

questions.question: ARM SVE differs from x86 AVX primarily because:
options: SVE uses 64-bit registers while AVX uses 256-bit
SVE vector length is determined at runtime by the hardware, not fixed at compile time
SVE only supports integer operations, not floating point
SVE requires all data to be 64-byte aligned
questions.explanation: SVE is vector-length agnostic (VLA): the hardware chooses the vector register width (128-2048 bits). The same binary runs optimally on any SVE implementation.

questions.question: Why must data be aligned to the SIMD register width for best performance?
options: Unaligned access causes a segmentation fault
Aligned loads can fetch the entire vector in one memory transaction; unaligned loads may span two cache lines and cost extra cycles
The compiler refuses to vectorize unaligned data
Aligned data is stored in the TLB for faster lookup
questions.explanation: When data is aligned to the vector width, a single cache-line fetch provides the entire vector. Unaligned data may straddle two cache lines, requiring two loads and a merge — extra latency and potential cache pollution.

questions.question: What does SIMD stand for, and how does it improve performance?
options: Single Instruction Multiple Data — one instruction operates on multiple data elements simultaneously
Sequential Instruction Memory Design — instructions are reordered for efficiency
Static Instruction Mapping Database — a compiler optimization technique
Shared Instruction Multiplexing Device — a hardware virtualization feature
questions.explanation: SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) applies one operation to a vector of data elements in parallel. For example, AVX-512 can add 16 floats simultaneously. This is ideal for data-parallel workloads like image processing, matrix multiplication, and scientific computing.
